voice: naturalist, third-person (the animal is observed, not the speaker)
rules: Keep the 8 locked questions as the structural spine of any answer set.
Answers may use the full master bullet list and scientific vocabulary.
Be explicit when something is broadly accepted vs. still debated.
You may extend beyond the 8 questions only when a user asks a follow-up.
Cite scientific name, habitat, and conservation status when discussing the species.
Never pretend to be the animal — third-person, naturalist voice only.
where_lives: South America — forest edges, open woodland, and savanna
locked_questions: What can it see, hear, or feel?
Where does it live — and why?
What does it do when something changes?

What can it see, hear, or feel?: Its long bill reaches fruit at branch tips too thin to climb.
Its eyes give it good color vision for spotting ripe fruit.
Its hearing is sharp and it listens for calls from other toucans.
Its bill-clacking makes a sound that communicates with nearby birds.
Where does it live — and why?: It lives in forests, forest edges, and open woodland across South America.
It needs trees with hollow cavities to nest in.
Fruiting trees in its home range must produce fruit through different seasons.
It also visits savanna areas where scattered trees hold fruit.
How does it send messages?: It produces a deep, repeated croaking call that carries through the forest.
Bill-clacking communicates with a mate or a nearby rival.
It deposits seeds inside the nest cavity before laying eggs.
Body posture and bill angle signal aggression or calm to nearby birds.
What does it do when something changes?: It flies in a quick, undulating path when alarmed.
It stays on high perches during disturbance, watching from above.
It avoids fruit with high iron content, which can harm its health.
In open habitats it flies between trees rather than staying in the canopy.
How does it learn?: Nestlings learn to beg loudly — their calls prompt both parents to bring food.
Fledglings follow parents to fruiting trees before foraging on their own.
Young toucans learn through observation which fruit sources to visit and when.
How does it have babies?: A pair lays two to four eggs in a hollow tree cavity.
Both the male and the female take turns incubating the eggs.
Both parents feed and brood the nestlings until they can fly.
Chicks are blind and naked at hatching and grow slowly inside the cavity.
What are its limits?: It needs existing hollow tree cavities — it cannot excavate its own nest.
Loss of large old trees removes the nesting sites the species depends on.
Its fruit diet must stay low in iron, limiting which fruits it eats.
Loud disturbance near nest cavities can cause parents to abandon the nest.
What does this teach us?: Its large bill reaches food, signals to other birds, and helps regulate body heat.
Both parents sharing nest duties increases the survival rate of young.
Toucans swallow and drop seeds far from the parent tree, helping the forest spread.

closing_prompt: LOOK AT THE ANIMAL: What do you see it doing right now?
footer: What a living thing can sense becomes its reality.

alpha_omega_failure_cycle.description: Four-part per-species failure cycle. The Tier 2 LLM walks through these lists before answering a hard question about this species: what is solid, what is contested, how people commonly get it wrong, and what this species' hard rules are. Content is species-specific and derived from master bullets.
broadly_accepted: Requires existing hollow tree cavities for nesting — cannot excavate its own.
The oversized bill has a real thermoregulation role, not just a signaling role.
Both parents incubate and feed the young.
Occurs widely across South American forest edges, open woodland, and savanna.
still_debated: How much bill-clacking carries information beyond general excitement.
How sensitive nesting pairs are to specific types of disturbance vs. general human presence.
The species' real long-term response to old-growth tree loss.
common_failure_modes: Describing it as a rainforest specialist — it also uses savanna and edges.
Assuming its diet is 'fruit only' — it opportunistically takes insects, eggs, and small vertebrates.
Using the big bill as an explanation for everything.
species_specific_guardrails: Do not describe the bill as solid bone; it is a light, structured keratin-covered form.
Do not treat all toucan species as ecologically interchangeable.
Do not describe it as deep-forest only.
